{{日本の寺院 |名称 =妙覚寺 |画像 = Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan Gate.jpg |画像説明 =
{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |所在地 =[[東京都]][[江戸川区]][[一之江]]六丁目19番10号 | 緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 41 | 緯度秒 = 25.6 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 52 | 経度秒 = 38.3 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP |山号 = 金嶋山 |院号 = |宗旨 = [[日蓮宗]] |宗派 = |寺格 = |本尊 = |創建年 = [[弘安]]7年（[[1284年]]） |開山 = 等覚院阿闍梨日全 |開基 = |中興年 = |中興 = |正式名 = 金嶋山 妙覚寺 |別称 = |札所等 = |文化財 = |公式HP = |公式HP名 = |地図 =Tokyo city#Japan }} '''妙覚寺'''（みょうかくじ）は、[[東京都]][[江戸川区]][[一之江]]六丁目にある[[日蓮宗]]の寺院である。[[山号]]は金嶋山。 == 概要 == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|本殿]] [[弘安]]7年（[[1284年]]）に[[等覚]]院[[阿闍梨]]日全が開山したため等覚院とも称す[[#江戸川区史|江戸川区史 (1976)]], pp. 522-523。江戸川区の地において最初に開かれた日蓮宗寺院である{{efn2|江戸川区一之江の[[感応寺 (江戸川区)|感應寺]]は元久2年（1205年）に開山した寺院だが、当初は真言宗で、正応元年（1288年）に身延3世・日進が日蓮宗に改宗したと伝わる。また同区篠崎の[[妙勝寺 (江戸川区上篠崎町)|妙勝寺]]は弘安2年（1279年）に浅草寺の住職・寂海法師（日寂）が日蓮宗に改宗して開山したと伝わるが、当初は浅草の石浜にあり江戸川区に移ったのは日寂が没した弘安9年（1287年）以降、2世の代である[[#江戸川区史|江戸川区史 (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|江戸川区の仏像・仏画2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}}。旧本山は[[千葉県]][[市川市]][[中山 (市川市)|中山]]にある日蓮宗の大本山・[[法華経寺|正中山法華経寺]]{{R|edogawakushi522}}。開祖・日全は武将・[[千葉頼胤|千葉介頼胤]]の甥で{{efn2|妙覚寺入口付近の案内板「妙覚寺史跡」による。1986年（昭和61年）12月に妙覚寺34世・露木日真が記述した。2018年（平成30年）3月観覧。}}、法華経寺2世・[[日高 (僧)|日高]]の弟子とも、3世・[[日祐]]の弟子ともされる[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], p. 619。かつては寺中に梅林院、正運坊、千林坊などを擁した。[[六老僧]]の1人・[[日朗]]の作と記された日蓮木造座像を本堂内に安置する。寺宝として所蔵する二幅の紙本墨書大[[法華曼荼羅|曼荼羅]]（日祐筆と日全筆）は江戸川区の[[登録有形文化財]]。境内には日蓮の銅像と[[妙見菩薩]]を祀った妙見堂がある{{R|edogawakushi522}}。 == 開山・日全 == 弘安7年（1284年）5月に妙覚寺を開山した日全は等覚院と号し、[[鎌倉時代]]の[[武将]]・[[千葉氏|千葉]]介6祖・[[千葉頼胤|頼胤]]の甥して生まれ、[[少納言]][[律師]]であった。日全は千葉県市川市中山にある大本山・正中山法華経寺から形成された流派・中山門流（日常門流）の僧で、法華経寺2世・[[日高 (僧)|日高]]の弟子とも、3世・[[日祐]]の弟子ともされる{{R|nichirenshujiten619}}。法華経寺を開山した[[日常 (僧)|日常]]こと[[富木常忍]]は、日全の叔父・千葉介頼胤の家臣で{{kotobank|富木常忍}}、法華経寺3世・日祐は頼胤の孫・[[千葉胤貞]]の養子である{{kotobank|日祐}}{{kotobank|千葉氏}}。 法華経寺3世・日祐は日蓮宗の祖山とされる[[身延山]][[久遠寺]]3世・[[日進 (身延3世)|日進]]と親交を深めたが[[#身延山史|身延山史 (1973)]], pp. 39-44、日全もまた日進に師事して抄物・相伝・口決を相承したという。この時代、中山法華経寺と身延山久遠寺は親密に交流を図っており、共に蔵書を書写し補充したと推測される。また日全は[[比叡山延暦寺|比叡山]]および仙波に遊学して[[天台宗|天台]]学も学んだとされ、代表的な著書に[[正慶]]から[[康永]]3年（[[1344年]]）5月にかけて今島田[[唱行寺 (市川市)|唱行寺]]で述作したという「法華問答正義抄」22巻があり、中山法華経寺の宝庫に所蔵されている。日全は康永3年（1344年）5月25日に没した。生年、年齢は不明{{R|nichirenshujiten619}}[[#全国寺院名鑑|全国寺院名鑑 (1969)]], p. 東京都-79。 == 妙見堂 == 妙見堂は[[北斗七星]]を神格化した[[妙見菩薩]]を祀った堂で、妙見菩薩は尊星王、妙見尊星王、北辰菩薩とも称され、国土を守って災難を除き、寿命を延ばす福徳があるとされる{{kotobank|妙見菩薩}}。妙見信仰は日本では[[平安時代]]以来、[[京都]]幾内に多かったが、[[中世]]では[[武家]]の[[守護神]]として[[千葉氏]]、[[大内氏]]、[[名和氏]]、[[相馬氏]]ら地方[[豪族]]に[[帰依]]された。また[[中国]]で昊天上帝と馬歩神が[[冬至]]に祭られたことから日本では守護神、[[軍神]]の他に馬の神としても尊崇された[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], pp. 393-394。 [[江戸時代|江戸]]後期に[[江戸幕府|幕府]]が編集した地誌「[[新編武蔵風土記稿]]」によれば、[[貞治]]元年（[[1362年]]）に[[旧江戸川]]の[[妙見島]]（江戸川区[[東葛西]]3丁目）にあった妙見堂に千葉氏尊崇の妙見菩薩像を祀り、その後、小松川を経て一之江の妙覚寺に妙見像を移したとされるが{{Sfnp|蘆田|1996|p=101}}{{Sfnp|蘆田|1929|p=101}}、現存はしていない。現在の妙見堂は[[昭和]]期、妙覚寺34世・日真の代に建立、妙覚寺では年中行事として12月22日の[[冬至]]の日に「妙見さまの星まつり」を行っている{{efn2|前述した妙覚寺入口付近の案内板「妙覚寺史跡」による。2018年（平成30年）3月観覧。}}。 妙見菩薩は北辰菩薩とも称するが、北辰の名は[[幕末]]の剣豪・[[千葉周作]]が創始した剣術の流派「[[北辰一刀流]]」にもみられる。北辰一刀流は[[坂本龍馬]]が学んだことでも知られる{{kotobank|北辰一刀流}}。 == 第六天堂 == 一之江三丁目に妙覚寺の飛地があり[[第六天]]堂が建つ。[[応安]]元年（[[1368年]]）に妙覚寺2世・日典が中山法華経寺3世・日祐を招いて第六天を勧請したとされ、妙覚寺所蔵の「妙応第六尊天略縁起」に詳しく記されている{{R|edogawakushi522}}。 仏教の宇宙論には人々がいる迷い世界を欲界、色界、無色界の3種に分けた[[三界]]があり{{kotobank|三界}}、第六天とは欲界の6つの天の頂上・[[他化自在天]]のことで、そこには第六天の魔王・[[天魔]]破旬がいるとされる。天魔は人々の仏道を妨げ悪業をなさしめるという[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], p. 280。日蓮は末法救済の本尊として[[大曼荼羅]]を著し、[[題目]]「[[南無妙法蓮華経]]」の文字を中心に仏や菩薩などの名を連ねたが、その中に第六天魔王の名も記した。日蓮は曼荼羅に第六天魔王を含む九界の主の名を連ねて九界が皆等しく[[成仏]]する[[浄土]]を著わしたとされ、[[法華経]]に帰依することで第六天魔王でさえも仏に成るという[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], pp. 262-264{{efn2|九界の主とは、欲界の主・梵天、三十三天の主・釈提桓因（帝釈）、三界の魔王・第六天魔王、閻浮提の三光天子・日月明星、人界の主・輪王、摩竭陀の主・阿闍世、修羅界・阿修羅、畜生界・竜王、地獄界・提婆をいう{{R|nichirenshujiten262}}。}}。 第六天は[[戦国武将]]・[[織田信長]]が自らを第六天魔王と称したことでも知られる{{Sfnp|藤巻|2001}}。 == 文化財 == === 紙本墨書大曼荼羅 2幅（日祐筆・日全筆） === {{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |所在地 =[[東京都]][[江戸川区]][[一之江]]六丁目19番10号 | 緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 41 | 緯度秒 = 25.6 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 52 |公式HP名 = |地図 =Tokyo city#Japan }} '''妙覚寺'''（みょうかくじ）は、[[東京都]][[江戸川区]][[一之江]]六丁目にある[[日蓮宗]]の寺院である。[[山号]]は金嶋山。 == 概要 == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|本殿]] [[弘安]]7年（[[1284年]]）に[[等覚]]院[[阿闍梨]]日全が開山したため等覚院とも称す[[#江戸川区史|江戸川区史 (1976)]], pp. 522-523。江戸川区の地において最初に開かれた日蓮宗寺院である{{efn2|江戸川区一之江の[[感応寺 (江戸川区)|感應寺]]は元久2年（1205年）に開山した寺院だが、当初は真言宗で、正応元年（1288年）に身延3世・日進が日蓮宗に改宗したと伝わる。また同区篠崎の[[妙勝寺 (江戸川区上篠崎町)|妙勝寺]]は弘安2年（1279年）に浅草寺の住職・寂海法師（日寂）が日蓮宗に改宗して開山したと伝わるが、当初は浅草の石浜にあり江戸川区に移ったのは日寂が没した弘安9年（1287年）以降、2世の代である[[#江戸川区史|江戸川区史 (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|江戸川区の仏像・仏画2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}}。旧本山は[[千葉県]][[市川市]][[中山 (市川市)|中山]]にある日蓮宗の大本山・[[法華経寺|正中山法華経寺]]{{R|edogawakushi522}}。開祖・日全は武将・[[千葉頼胤|千葉介頼胤]]の甥で{{efn2|妙覚寺入口付近の案内板「妙覚寺史跡」による。1986年（昭和61年）12月に妙覚寺34世・露木日真が記述した。2018年（平成30年）3月観覧。}}、法華経寺2世・[[日高 (僧)|日高]]の弟子とも、3世・[[日祐]]の弟子ともされる[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], p. 619。かつては寺中に梅林院、正運坊、千林坊などを擁した。[[六老僧]]の1人・[[日朗]]の作と記された日蓮木造座像を本堂内に安置する。寺宝として所蔵する二幅の紙本墨書大[[法華曼荼羅|曼荼羅]]（日祐筆と日全筆）は江戸川区の[[登録有形文化財]]。境内には日蓮の銅像と[[妙見菩薩]]を祀った妙見堂がある{{R|edogawakushi522}}。 == 開山・日全 == 弘安7年（1284年）5月に妙覚寺を開山した日全は等覚院と号し、[[鎌倉時代]]の[[武将]]・[[千葉氏|千葉]]介6祖・[[千葉頼胤|頼胤]]の甥して生まれ、[[少納言]][[律師]]であった。日全は千葉県市川市中山にある大本山・正中山法華経寺から形成された流派・中山門流（日常門流）の僧で、法華経寺2世・[[日高 (僧)|日高]]の弟子とも、3世・[[日祐]]の弟子ともされる{{R|nichirenshujiten619}}。法華経寺を開山した[[日常 (僧)|日常]]こと[[富木常忍]]は、日全の叔父・千葉介頼胤の家臣で{{kotobank|富木常忍}}、法華経寺3世・日祐は頼胤の孫・[[千葉胤貞]]の養子である{{kotobank|日祐}}{{kotobank|千葉氏}}。 法華経寺3世・日祐は日蓮宗の祖山とされる[[身延山]][[久遠寺]]3世・[[日進 (身延3世)|日進]]と親交を深めたが[[#身延山史|身延山史 (1973)]], pp. 39-44、日全もまた日進に師事して抄物・相伝・口決を相承したという。この時代、中山法華経寺と身延山久遠寺は親密に交流を図っており、共に蔵書を書写し補充したと推測される。また日全は[[比叡山延暦寺|比叡山]]および仙波に遊学して[[天台宗|天台]]学も学んだとされ、代表的な著書に[[正慶]]から[[康永]]3年（[[1344年]]）5月にかけて今島田[[唱行寺 (市川市)|唱行寺]]で述作したという「法華問答正義抄」22巻があり、中山法華経寺の宝庫に所蔵されている。日全は康永3年（1344年）5月25日に没した。生年、年齢は不明{{R|nichirenshujiten619}}[[#全国寺院名鑑|全国寺院名鑑 (1969)]], p. 東京都-79。 == 妙見堂 == 妙見堂は[[北斗七星]]を神格化した[[妙見菩薩]]を祀った堂で、妙見菩薩は尊星王、妙見尊星王、北辰菩薩とも称され、国土を守って災難を除き、寿命を延ばす福徳があるとされる{{kotobank|妙見菩薩}}。妙見信仰は日本では[[平安時代]]以来、[[京都]]幾内に多かったが、[[中世]]では[[武家]]の[[守護神]]として[[千葉氏]]、[[大内氏]]、[[名和氏]]、[[相馬氏]]ら地方[[豪族]]に[[帰依]]された。また[[中国]]で昊天上帝と馬歩神が[[冬至]]に祭られたことから日本では守護神、[[軍神]]の他に馬の神としても尊崇された[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], pp. 393-394。 [[江戸時代|江戸]]後期に[[江戸幕府|幕府]]が編集した地誌「[[新編武蔵風土記稿]]」によれば、[[貞治]]元年（[[1362年]]）に[[旧江戸川]]の[[妙見島]]（江戸川区[[東葛西]]3丁目）にあった妙見堂に千葉氏尊崇の妙見菩薩像を祀り、その後、小松川を経て一之江の妙覚寺に妙見像を移したとされるが{{Sfnp|蘆田|1996|p=101}}{{Sfnp|蘆田|1929|p=101}}、現存はしていない。現在の妙見堂は[[昭和]]期、妙覚寺34世・日真の代に建立、妙覚寺では年中行事として12月22日の[[冬至]]の日に「妙見さまの星まつり」を行っている{{efn2|前述した妙覚寺入口付近の案内板「妙覚寺史跡」による。2018年（平成30年）3月観覧。}}。 妙見菩薩は北辰菩薩とも称するが、北辰の名は[[幕末]]の剣豪・[[千葉周作]]が創始した剣術の流派「[[北辰一刀流]]」にもみられる。北辰一刀流は[[坂本龍馬]]が学んだことでも知られる{{kotobank|北辰一刀流}}。 == 第六天堂 == 一之江三丁目に妙覚寺の飛地があり[[第六天]]堂が建つ。[[応安]]元年（[[1368年]]）に妙覚寺2世・日典が中山法華経寺3世・日祐を招いて第六天を勧請したとされ、妙覚寺所蔵の「妙応第六尊天略縁起」に詳しく記されている{{R|edogawakushi522}}。 仏教の宇宙論には人々がいる迷い世界を欲界、色界、無色界の3種に分けた[[三界]]があり{{kotobank|三界}}、第六天とは欲界の6つの天の頂上・[[他化自在天]]のことで、そこには第六天の魔王・[[天魔]]破旬がいるとされる。天魔は人々の仏道を妨げ悪業をなさしめるという[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], p. 280。日蓮は末法救済の本尊として[[大曼荼羅]]を著し、[[題目]]「[[南無妙法蓮華経]]」の文字を中心に仏や菩薩などの名を連ねたが、その中に第六天魔王の名も記した。日蓮は曼荼羅に第六天魔王を含む九界の主の名を連ねて九界が皆等しく[[成仏]]する[[浄土]]を著わしたとされ、[[法華経]]に帰依することで第六天魔王でさえも仏に成るという[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], pp. 262-264{{efn2|九界の主とは、欲界の主・梵天、三十三天の主・釈提桓因（帝釈）、三界の魔王・第六天魔王、閻浮提の三光天子・日月明星、人界の主・輪王、摩竭陀の主・阿闍世、修羅界・阿修羅、畜生界・竜王、地獄界・提婆をいう{{R|nichirenshujiten262}}。}}。 第六天は[[戦国武将]]・[[織田信長]]が自らを第六天魔王と称したことでも知られる{{Sfnp|藤巻|2001}}。 == 文化財 == === 紙本墨書大曼荼羅 2幅（日祐筆・日全筆） === [[1984年]]（昭和59年）に2幅の「紙本墨書大曼荼羅」が江戸川区の有形文化財・歴史資料に登録された。1幅は中山法華経寺3世・日祐から妙覚寺2世・日典に授けられたとする[[応安]]元年（[[1368年]]）の墨書曼荼羅、もう1幅は妙覚寺開祖・日全から弟子に授けられた[[嘉暦]]2年（[[1327年]]）の墨書曼荼羅である{{R|nichiyuhitsu}}。 === 第六天の石造道標 === [[1983年]]（昭和58年）一之江三丁目の第六天堂にある「第六天の石造道標」が江戸川区の有形文化財・歴史資料に登録された。これは[[文政]]9年（[[1826年]]）に建てた角柱型の[[道標]]で、もとは現在の[[東京都道318号環状七号線|環状7号線]]（旧行徳道中井堀橋）と今井街道が交差する付近にあった。道標の正面には第六天堂への道筋を示す文字「妙応第六天道、是ヨリ左リ」が刻まれている。 一之江六丁目の妙覚寺には寺宝として中山法華経寺2世・日高が著したとする第六天に関する消息断片が所蔵されている{{R|edogawakushi522}}。また第六天堂には樹齢500年以上の[[エノキ]]があり江戸川区の天然記念物だったが[[#江戸川区の文化財|江戸川区の文化財 (1982)]], p. 36、現存しない。 == 一之江保育園 == 一之江[[保育園]]は[[1952年]]（昭和27年）に妙覚寺34世・日真が開設、[[宗教法人]]妙覚寺の経営による[[東京都]]公認の[[児童福祉施設]]である{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=一之江保育園|work=一之江保育園公式サイト|publisher=妙覚寺|accessdate=2018-04-15}}。 == 近隣の日蓮宗寺院 == * [[長勝寺 (江戸川区)|長勝寺]]（江戸川区一之江6丁目）妙覚寺の塔頭だったが[[天正]]11年（[[1583年]]）に妙覚寺9世・日住の弟子、日信が開山した{{R|edogawakushi522}}。 * [[感応寺 (江戸川区)|感應寺]]（江戸川区一之江7丁目）[[真言宗]]の寺院だったが[[正応]]元年（[[1288年]]）に妙覚寺の開祖・日全が師事した身延山[[久遠寺]]3世・[[日進 (身延3世)|日進]]が日蓮宗に改宗した{{R|edogawakushi524}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist2}} === 出典 === |公式HP名 = |地図 =Tokyo city#Japan }} '''妙覚寺'''（みょうかくじ）は、[[東京都]][[江戸川区]][[一之江]]六丁目にある[[日蓮宗]]の寺院である。[[山号]]は金嶋山。 == 概要 == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|本殿]] [[弘安]]7年（[[1284年]]）に[[等覚]]院[[阿闍梨]]日全が開山したため等覚院とも称す[[#江戸川区史|江戸川区史 (1976)]], pp. 522-523。江戸川区の地において最初に開かれた日蓮宗寺院である{{efn2|江戸川区一之江の[[感応寺 (江戸川区)|感應寺]]は元久2年（1205年）に開山した寺院だが、当初は真言宗で、正応元年（1288年）に身延3世・日進が日蓮宗に改宗したと伝わる。また同区篠崎の[[妙勝寺 (江戸川区上篠崎町)|妙勝寺]]は弘安2年（1279年）に浅草寺の住職・寂海法師（日寂）が日蓮宗に改宗して開山したと伝わるが、当初は浅草の石浜にあり江戸川区に移ったのは日寂が没した弘安9年（1287年）以降、2世の代である[[#江戸川区史|江戸川区史 (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|江戸川区の仏像・仏画2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}}。旧本山は[[千葉県]][[市川市]][[中山 (市川市)|中山]]にある日蓮宗の大本山・[[法華経寺|正中山法華経寺]]{{R|edogawakushi522}}。開祖・日全は武将・[[千葉頼胤|千葉介頼胤]]の甥で{{efn2|妙覚寺入口付近の案内板「妙覚寺史跡」による。1986年（昭和61年）12月に妙覚寺34世・露木日真が記述した。2018年（平成30年）3月観覧。}}、法華経寺2世・[[日高 (僧)|日高]]の弟子とも、3世・[[日祐]]の弟子ともされる[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], p. 619。かつては寺中に梅林院、正運坊、千林坊などを擁した。[[六老僧]]の1人・[[日朗]]の作と記された日蓮木造座像を本堂内に安置する。寺宝として所蔵する二幅の紙本墨書大[[法華曼荼羅|曼荼羅]]（日祐筆と日全筆）は江戸川区の[[登録有形文化財]]。境内には日蓮の銅像と[[妙見菩薩]]を祀った妙見堂がある{{R|edogawakushi522}}。 == 開山・日全 == 弘安7年（1284年）5月に妙覚寺を開山した日全は等覚院と号し、[[鎌倉時代]]の[[武将]]・[[千葉氏|千葉]]介6祖・[[千葉頼胤|頼胤]]の甥して生まれ、[[少納言]][[律師]]であった。日全は千葉県市川市中山にある大本山・正中山法華経寺から形成された流派・中山門流（日常門流）の僧で、法華経寺2世・[[日高 (僧)|日高]]の弟子とも、3世・[[日祐]]の弟子ともされる{{R|nichirenshujiten619}}。法華経寺を開山した[[日常 (僧)|日常]]こと[[富木常忍]]は、日全の叔父・千葉介頼胤の家臣で{{kotobank|富木常忍}}、法華経寺3世・日祐は頼胤の孫・[[千葉胤貞]]の養子である{{kotobank|日祐}}{{kotobank|千葉氏}}。 法華経寺3世・日祐は日蓮宗の祖山とされる[[身延山]][[久遠寺]]3世・[[日進 (身延3世)|日進]]と親交を深めたが[[#身延山史|身延山史 (1973)]], pp. 39-44、日全もまた日進に師事して抄物・相伝・口決を相承したという。この時代、中山法華経寺と身延山久遠寺は親密に交流を図っており、共に蔵書を書写し補充したと推測される。また日全は[[比叡山延暦寺|比叡山]]および仙波に遊学して[[天台宗|天台]]学も学んだとされ、代表的な著書に[[正慶]]から[[康永]]3年（[[1344年]]）5月にかけて今島田[[唱行寺 (市川市)|唱行寺]]で述作したという「法華問答正義抄」22巻があり、中山法華経寺の宝庫に所蔵されている。日全は康永3年（1344年）5月25日に没した。生年、年齢は不明{{R|nichirenshujiten619}}[[#全国寺院名鑑|全国寺院名鑑 (1969)]], p. 東京都-79。 == 妙見堂 == 妙見堂は[[北斗七星]]を神格化した[[妙見菩薩]]を祀った堂で、妙見菩薩は尊星王、妙見尊星王、北辰菩薩とも称され、国土を守って災難を除き、寿命を延ばす福徳があるとされる{{kotobank|妙見菩薩}}。妙見信仰は日本では[[平安時代]]以来、[[京都]]幾内に多かったが、[[中世]]では[[武家]]の[[守護神]]として[[千葉氏]]、[[大内氏]]、[[名和氏]]、[[相馬氏]]ら地方[[豪族]]に[[帰依]]された。また[[中国]]で昊天上帝と馬歩神が[[冬至]]に祭られたことから日本では守護神、[[軍神]]の他に馬の神としても尊崇された[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], pp. 393-394。 [[江戸時代|江戸]]後期に[[江戸幕府|幕府]]が編集した地誌「[[新編武蔵風土記稿]]」によれば、[[貞治]]元年（[[1362年]]）に[[旧江戸川]]の[[妙見島]]（江戸川区[[東葛西]]3丁目）にあった妙見堂に千葉氏尊崇の妙見菩薩像を祀り、その後、小松川を経て一之江の妙覚寺に妙見像を移したとされるが{{Sfnp|蘆田|1996|p=101}}{{Sfnp|蘆田|1929|p=101}}、現存はしていない。現在の妙見堂は[[昭和]]期、妙覚寺34世・日真の代に建立、妙覚寺では年中行事として12月22日の[[冬至]]の日に「妙見さまの星まつり」を行っている{{efn2|前述した妙覚寺入口付近の案内板「妙覚寺史跡」による。2018年（平成30年）3月観覧。}}。 妙見菩薩は北辰菩薩とも称するが、北辰の名は[[幕末]]の剣豪・[[千葉周作]]が創始した剣術の流派「[[北辰一刀流]]」にもみられる。北辰一刀流は[[坂本龍馬]]が学んだことでも知られる{{kotobank|北辰一刀流}}。 == 第六天堂 == 一之江三丁目に妙覚寺の飛地があり[[第六天]]堂が建つ。[[応安]]元年（[[1368年]]）に妙覚寺2世・日典が中山法華経寺3世・日祐を招いて第六天を勧請したとされ、妙覚寺所蔵の「妙応第六尊天略縁起」に詳しく記されている{{R|edogawakushi522}}。 仏教の宇宙論には人々がいる迷い世界を欲界、色界、無色界の3種に分けた[[三界]]があり{{kotobank|三界}}、第六天とは欲界の6つの天の頂上・[[他化自在天]]のことで、そこには第六天の魔王・[[天魔]]破旬がいるとされる。天魔は人々の仏道を妨げ悪業をなさしめるという[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], p. 280。日蓮は末法救済の本尊として[[大曼荼羅]]を著し、[[題目]]「[[南無妙法蓮華経]]」の文字を中心に仏や菩薩などの名を連ねたが、その中に第六天魔王の名も記した。日蓮は曼荼羅に第六天魔王を含む九界の主の名を連ねて九界が皆等しく[[成仏]]する[[浄土]]を著わしたとされ、[[法華経]]に帰依することで第六天魔王でさえも仏に成るという[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], pp. 262-264{{efn2|九界の主とは、欲界の主・梵天、三十三天の主・釈提桓因（帝釈）、三界の魔王・第六天魔王、閻浮提の三光天子・日月明星、人界の主・輪王、摩竭陀の主・阿闍世、修羅界・阿修羅、畜生界・竜王、地獄界・提婆をいう{{R|nichirenshujiten262}}。}}。 第六天は[[戦国武将]]・[[織田信長]]が自らを第六天魔王と称したことでも知られる{{Sfnp|藤巻|2001}}。 == 文化財 == === 紙本墨書大曼荼羅 2幅（日祐筆・日全筆） === [[1984年]]（昭和59年）に2幅の「紙本墨書大曼荼羅」が江戸川区の有形文化財・歴史資料に登録された。1幅は中山法華経寺3世・日祐から妙覚寺2世・日典に授けられたとする[[応安]]元年（[[1368年]]）の墨書曼荼羅、もう1幅は妙覚寺開祖・日全から弟子に授けられた[[嘉暦]]2年（[[1327年]]）の墨書曼荼羅である{{R|nichiyuhitsu}}。 === 第六天の石造道標 === [[1983年]]（昭和58年）一之江三丁目の第六天堂にある「第六天の石造道標」が江戸川区の有形文化財・歴史資料に登録された。これは[[文政]]9年（[[1826年]]）に建てた角柱型の[[道標]]で、もとは現在の[[東京都道318号環状七号線|環状7号線]]（旧行徳道中井堀橋）と今井街道が交差する付近にあった。道標の正面には第六天堂への道筋を示す文字「妙応第六天道、是ヨリ左リ」が刻まれている。 一之江六丁目の妙覚寺には寺宝として中山法華経寺2世・日高が著したとする第六天に関する消息断片が所蔵されている{{R|edogawakushi522}}。また第六天堂には樹齢500年以上の[[エノキ]]があり江戸川区の天然記念物だったが[[#江戸川区の文化財|江戸川区の文化財 (1982)]], p. 36、現存しない。 == 交通アクセス == *[[一之江駅]]より徒歩10分（{{OSM経路図|start=35.68606,139.88345|goal=35.69042,139.87737|name=経路案内}}）。 == 一之江保育園 == 一之江[[保育園]]は[[1952年]]（昭和27年）に妙覚寺34世・日真が開設、[[宗教法人]]妙覚寺の経営による[[東京都]]公認の[[児童福祉施設]]である{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=一之江保育園|work=一之江保育園公式サイト|publisher=妙覚寺|accessdate=2018-04-15}}。 == 近隣の日蓮宗寺院 == * [[長勝寺 (江戸川区)|長勝寺]]（江戸川区一之江6丁目）妙覚寺の塔頭だったが[[天正]]11年（[[1583年]]）に妙覚寺9世・日住の弟子、日信が開山した{{R|edogawakushi522}}。 * [[感応寺 (江戸川区)|感應寺]]（江戸川区一之江7丁目）[[真言宗]]の寺院だったが[[正応]]元年（[[1288年]]）に妙覚寺の開祖・日全が師事した身延山[[久遠寺]]3世・[[日進 (身延3世)|日進]]が日蓮宗に改宗した{{R|edogawakushi524}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist2}} === 出典 === {{Reflist|2|refs= *{{Cite web|url= https://www.city.edogawa.tokyo.jp/e_bunkazai/bunkazai/toroku/076.html|title=日祐筆紙本墨書大曼荼羅（一幅） 日全筆紙本墨書大曼荼羅（一幅）|work=江戸川区公式サイト|publisher=江戸川区|accessdate=2020-04-17|date=2019-1-31}} {{Cite web|url=https://www.city.edogawa.tokyo.jp/e_bunkazai/bunkazai/toroku/060.html|title=第六天の石造道標|work=江戸川区公式サイト|publisher=江戸川区|accessdate=2020-04-16|date=2019-1-31}} }} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|volume=第3巻|editor=江戸川区区史編纂室|title=江戸川区史|publisher=江戸川区|date=1976|id={{全国書誌番号|73015027}}|ref=江戸川区史}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|editor=江戸川区仏教美術調査団|publisher=江戸川区教育委員会|date=2013-03|id={{全国書誌番号|23016500}}|ref=江戸川区の仏像・仏画2}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=日蓮宗事典|editor=日蓮宗事典刊行委員会|publisher=日蓮宗宗務院|date=1981-10|id={{全国書誌番号|82021590}}|ref=日蓮宗事典}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=身延山史・続身延山史|editor=身延山久遠寺|publisher=身延山久遠寺|date=1973|id={{全国書誌番号|74004054}}|ref=身延山史}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=全国寺院名鑑|editor=全日本仏教会寺院名鑑刊行会|publisher=全日本仏教会寺院名鑑刊行会|date=1969-03|id={{全国書誌番号|74006510}}|ref=全国寺院名鑑}} * {{Cite book|和書|volume=第2巻|isbn=4639000170|series=[[大日本地誌大系]] 8|title=[[新編武蔵風土記稿]]|author1=蘆田伊人|authorlink1=蘆田伊人|author2=根本誠二|authorlink2=根本誠二|publisher=[[雄山閣]]|date=1996-06|edition=第2版|ref={{SfnRef|蘆田|1996}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第6巻 新編武蔵風土記稿2|chapter=巻ノ28葛飾郡ノ9東一之江村|id={{NDLJP|1214842/58}}|publisher=雄山閣|editor=蘆田伊人|date=1929-8|page=101|ref={{SfnRef|蘆田|1929}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=江戸川区の文化財|editor=江戸川区教育委員会社会教育課|publisher=江戸川区教育委員会|date=1982-03|ncid=BN0275712X|ref=江戸川区の文化財}} * {{Cite book|和書|isbn=405901043X|title=第六天魔王信長|last=藤巻|first=一保|publisher=[[学習研究社]]|series=[[学研M文庫]]|date=2001-03|ref=harv}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat}} {{Buddhism2}} {{デフォルトソート:みようかくし えとかわ}} [[Category:江戸川区の寺]] (僧)|日高]]の弟子とも、3世・[[日祐]]の弟子ともされる{{R|nichirenshujiten619}}。法華経寺を開山した[[日常 (僧)|日常]]こと[[富木常忍]]は、日全の叔父・千葉介頼胤の家臣で{{kotobank|富木常忍}}、法華経寺3世・日祐は頼胤の孫・[[千葉胤貞]]の養子である{{kotobank|日祐}}{{kotobank|千葉氏}}。 法華経寺3世・日祐は日蓮宗の祖山とされる[[身延山]][[久遠寺]]3世・[[日進 (身延3世)|日進]]と親交を深めたが[[#身延山史|身延山史 (1973)]], pp. 39-44、日全もまた日進に師事して抄物・相伝・口決を相承したという。この時代、中山法華経寺と身延山久遠寺は親密に交流を図っており、共に蔵書を書写し補充したと推測される。また日全は[[比叡山延暦寺|比叡山]]および仙波に遊学して[[天台宗|天台]]学も学んだとされ、代表的な著書に[[正慶]]から[[康永]]3年（[[1344年]]）5月にかけて今島田[[唱行寺 (市川市)|唱行寺]]で述作したという「法華問答正義抄」22巻があり、中山法華経寺の宝庫に所蔵されている。日全は康永3年（1344年）5月25日に没した。生年、年齢は不明{{R|nichirenshujiten619}}[[#全国寺院名鑑|全国寺院名鑑 (1969)]], p. 東京都-79。 == 妙見堂 == 妙見堂は[[北斗七星]]を神格化した[[妙見菩薩]]を祀った堂で、妙見菩薩は尊星王、妙見尊星王、北辰菩薩とも称され、国土を守って災難を除き、寿命を延ばす福徳があるとされる{{kotobank|妙見菩薩}}。妙見信仰は日本では[[平安時代]]以来、[[京都]]幾内に多かったが、[[中世]]では[[武家]]の[[守護神]]として[[千葉氏]]、[[大内氏]]、[[名和氏]]、[[相馬氏]]ら地方[[豪族]]に[[帰依]]された。また[[中国]]で昊天上帝と馬歩神が[[冬至]]に祭られたことから日本では守護神、[[軍神]]の他に馬の神としても尊崇された[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], pp. 393-394。 [[江戸時代|江戸]]後期に[[江戸幕府|幕府]]が編集した地誌「[[新編武蔵風土記稿]]」によれば、[[貞治]]元年（[[1362年]]）に[[旧江戸川]]の[[妙見島]]（江戸川区[[東葛西]]3丁目）にあった妙見堂に千葉氏尊崇の妙見菩薩像を祀り、その後、小松川を経て一之江の妙覚寺に妙見像を移したとされるが{{Sfnp|蘆田|1996|p=101}}{{Sfnp|蘆田|1929|p=101}}、現存はしていない。現在の妙見堂は[[昭和]]期、妙覚寺34世・日真の代に建立、妙覚寺では年中行事として12月22日の[[冬至]]の日に「妙見さまの星まつり」を行っている{{efn2|前述した妙覚寺入口付近の案内板「妙覚寺史跡」による。2018年（平成30年）3月観覧。}}。 妙見菩薩は北辰菩薩とも称するが、北辰の名は[[幕末]]の剣豪・[[千葉周作]]が創始した剣術の流派「[[北辰一刀流]]」にもみられる。北辰一刀流は[[坂本龍馬]]が学んだことでも知られる{{kotobank|北辰一刀流}}。 == 第六天堂 == 一之江三丁目に妙覚寺の飛地があり[[第六天]]堂が建つ。[[応安]]元年（[[1368年]]）に妙覚寺2世・日典が中山法華経寺3世・日祐を招いて第六天を勧請したとされ、妙覚寺所蔵の「妙応第六尊天略縁起」に詳しく記されている{{R|edogawakushi522}}。 仏教の宇宙論には人々がいる迷い世界を欲界、色界、無色界の3種に分けた[[三界]]があり{{kotobank|三界}}、第六天とは欲界の6つの天の頂上・[[他化自在天]]のことで、そこには第六天の魔王・[[天魔]]破旬がいるとされる。天魔は人々の仏道を妨げ悪業をなさしめるという[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], p. 280。日蓮は末法救済の本尊として[[大曼荼羅]]を著し、[[題目]]「[[南無妙法蓮華経]]」の文字を中心に仏や菩薩などの名を連ねたが、その中に第六天魔王の名も記した。日蓮は曼荼羅に第六天魔王を含む九界の主の名を連ねて九界が皆等しく[[成仏]]する[[浄土]]を著わしたとされ、[[法華経]]に帰依することで第六天魔王でさえも仏に成るという[[#日蓮宗事典|日蓮宗事典 (1981)]], pp. 262-264{{efn2|九界の主とは、欲界の主・梵天、三十三天の主・釈提桓因（帝釈）、三界の魔王・第六天魔王、閻浮提の三光天子・日月明星、人界の主・輪王、摩竭陀の主・阿闍世、修羅界・阿修羅、畜生界・竜王、地獄界・提婆をいう{{R|nichirenshujiten262}}。}}。 第六天は[[戦国武将]]・[[織田信長]]が自らを第六天魔王と称したことでも知られる{{Sfnp|藤巻|2001}}。 == 文化財 == === 紙本墨書大曼荼羅 2幅（日祐筆・日全筆） === [[1984年]]（昭和59年）に2幅の「紙本墨書大曼荼羅」が江戸川区の有形文化財・歴史資料に登録された。1幅は中山法華経寺3世・日祐から妙覚寺2世・日典に授けられたとする[[応安]]元年（[[1368年]]）の墨書曼荼羅、もう1幅は妙覚寺開祖・日全から弟子に授けられた[[嘉暦]]2年（[[1327年]]）の墨書曼荼羅である{{R|nichiyuhitsu}}。 === 第六天の石造道標 === [[1983年]]（昭和58年）一之江三丁目の第六天堂にある「第六天の石造道標」が江戸川区の有形文化財・歴史資料に登録された。これは[[文政]]9年（[[1826年]]）に建てた角柱型の[[道標]]で、もとは現在の[[東京都道318号環状七号線|環状7号線]]（旧行徳道中井堀橋）と今井街道が交差する付近にあった。道標の正面には第六天堂への道筋を示す文字「妙応第六天道、是ヨリ左リ」が刻まれている。 一之江六丁目の妙覚寺には寺宝として中山法華経寺2世・日高が著したとする第六天に関する消息断片が所蔵されている{{R|edogawakushi522}}。また第六天堂には樹齢500年以上の[[エノキ]]があり江戸川区の天然記念物だったが[[#江戸川区の文化財|江戸川区の文化財 (1982)]], p. 36、現存しない。 == 交通アクセス == *[[一之江駅]]より徒歩10分（{{OSM経路図|start=35.68606,139.88345|goal=35.69042,139.87737|name=経路案内}}）。 == 一之江保育園 == 一之江[[保育園]]は[[1952年]]（昭和27年）に妙覚寺34世・日真が開設、[[宗教法人]]妙覚寺の経営による[[東京都]]公認の[[児童福祉施設]]である{{Cite web|和書|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=一之江保育園|work=一之江保育園公式サイト|publisher=妙覚寺|accessdate=2018-04-15}}。 == 近隣の日蓮宗寺院 == * [[長勝寺 (江戸川区)|長勝寺]]（江戸川区一之江6丁目）妙覚寺の塔頭だったが[[天正]]11年（[[1583年]]）に妙覚寺9世・日住の弟子、日信が開山した{{R|edogawakushi522}}。 * [[感応寺 (江戸川区)|感應寺]]（江戸川区一之江7丁目）[[真言宗]]の寺院だったが[[正応]]元年（[[1288年]]）に妙覚寺の開祖・日全が師事した身延山[[久遠寺]]3世・[[日進 (身延3世)|日進]]が日蓮宗に改宗した{{R|edogawakushi524}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist2}} === 出典 === {{Reflist|2|refs= *{{Cite web|和書|url= https://www.city.edogawa.tokyo.jp/e_bunkazai/bunkazai/toroku/076.html|title=日祐筆紙本墨書大曼荼羅（一幅） 日全筆紙本墨書大曼荼羅（一幅）|work=江戸川区公式サイト|publisher=江戸川区|accessdate=2020-04-17|date=2019-1-31}} {{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.edogawa.tokyo.jp/e_bunkazai/bunkazai/toroku/060.html|title=第六天の石造道標|work=江戸川区公式サイト|publisher=江戸川区|accessdate=2020-04-16|date=2019-1-31}} }} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|volume=第3巻|editor=江戸川区区史編纂室|title=江戸川区史|publisher=江戸川区|date=1976|id={{全国書誌番号|73015027}}|ref=江戸川区史}} * {{Cite 8|title=[[新編武蔵風土記稿]]|author1=蘆田伊人|authorlink1=蘆田伊人|author2=根本誠二|authorlink2=根本誠二|publisher=[[雄山閣]]|date=1996-06|edition=第2版|ref={{SfnRef|蘆田|1996}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第6巻 新編武蔵風土記稿2|chapter=巻ノ28葛飾郡ノ9東一之江村|id={{NDLJP|1214842/58}}|publisher=雄山閣|editor=蘆田伊人|date=1929-8|page=101|ref={{SfnRef|蘆田|1929}}}} * {{Cite {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōkaku-ji | image = Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan Gate.jpg | caption =
{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 6-19-10 Ichinoe, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan | latd = 35 |latm = 41 |lats = 25.6 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 52 |longs = 38.3 |longEW = E | coordinates = JP | mount = Kinshōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = 1284 (Kōan 7) | founder = Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen | alias = | cultural_properties = }} '''Myōkaku-ji''' (妙覚寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in {{ill|Ichinoe|ja|一之江}}, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kinshōzan. == Overview == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] The temple was founded in 1284 (Kōan 7) by {{ill|Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja|等覚院阿闍梨日全}}, and is also referred to as Tōkaku-in.[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], pp. 522-523 It is considered the first Nichiren sect temple established in Edogawa Ward.{{efn2|The nearby {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}} was founded in 1205 (Genkyū 2) as a Shingon sect temple and converted to Nichiren Buddhism in 1288 (Shōō 1). Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist Images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|ja|日高}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐}}.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日朗}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. [[en:Yatsushiro Shrine]] [[Category:Myōken Faith articles that need translation]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:Myōken Faith articles that need translation]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[ja:妙覚寺 (江戸川区)]]

Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist Images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|ja|日高}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐}}.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日朗}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōkaku-ji | image = Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan Gate.jpg | caption =
{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 6-19-10 Ichinoe, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan | latd = 35 |latm = 41 |lats = 25.6 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 52 |longs = 38.3 |longEW = E | coordinates = JP | mount = Kinshōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = 1284 (Kōan 7) | founder = Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen | alias = | cultural_properties = }} '''Myōkaku-ji''' (妙覚寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in {{ill|Ichinoe|ja|一之江}}, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kinshōzan. == Overview == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] The temple was founded in 1284 (Kōan 7) by {{ill|Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja|等覚院阿闍梨日全}}, and is also referred to as Tōkaku-in.[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], pp. 522-523 It is considered the first Nichiren sect temple established in Edogawa Ward.{{efn2|The nearby {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}} was founded in 1205 (Genkyū 2) as a Shingon sect temple and converted to Nichiren Buddhism in 1288 (Shōō 1). Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|ja|日高}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐}}.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日朗}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. [[de:Yatsushiro Shrine]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[ja:妙覚寺 (江戸川区)]]

Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kinshōzan. == Overview == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] The temple was founded in 1284 (Kōan 7) by {{ill|Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja|等覚院阿闍梨日全}}, and is also referred to as Tōkaku-in.[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], pp. 522-523 It is considered the first Nichiren sect temple established in Edogawa Ward.{{efn2|The nearby {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}} was founded in 1205 (Genkyū 2) as a Shingon sect temple and converted to Nichiren Buddhism in 1288 (Shōō 1). Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|ja|日高}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐}}.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日朗}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kinshōzan. == Overview == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] The temple was founded in 1284 (Kōan 7) by {{ill|Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja|等覚院阿闍梨日全}}, and is also referred to as Tōkaku-in.[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], pp. 522-523 It is considered the first Nichiren sect temple established in Edogawa Ward.{{efn2|The nearby {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}} was founded in 1205 (Genkyū 2) as a Shingon sect temple and converted to Nichiren Buddhism in 1288 (Shōō 1). Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|ja|日高}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐}}.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日朗}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. [[de:Yatsushiro Shrine]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[ja:妙覚寺 (江戸川区)]]

Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōkaku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan Gate.jpg | caption =
{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 6-19-10 Ichinoe, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan | latd = 35 |latm = 41 |lats = 25.6 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 52 |longs = 38.3 |longEW = E | coordinates = JP | mount = Kinshōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = 1284 (Kōan 7) | founder = Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen | alias = | cultural_properties = }} '''Myōkaku-ji''' (妙覚寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in {{ill|Ichinoe|ja|一之江}}, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kinshōzan. == Overview == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] The temple was founded in 1284 (Kōan 7) by {{ill|Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja|等覚院阿闍梨日全}}, and is also referred to as Tōkaku-in.[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], pp. 522-523 It is considered the first Nichiren sect temple established in Edogawa Ward.{{efn2|The nearby {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}} was founded in 1205 (Genkyū 2) as a Shingon sect temple and converted to Nichiren Buddhism in 1288 (Shōō 1). Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|ja|日高}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐}}.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日朗}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. [[de:Yatsushiro Shrine]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[category{Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[ja:妙覚寺 (江戸川区)]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōkaku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan Gate.jpg | caption =
{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 6-19-10 Ichinoe, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan | latd = 35 |latm = 41 |lats = 25.6 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 52 |longs = 38.3 |longEW = E | coordinates = JP | mount = Kinshōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = 1284 (Kōan 7) | founder = Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen | alias = | cultural_properties = }} '''Myōkaku-ji''' (妙覚寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in {{ill|Ichinoe|ja|一之江}}, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kinshōzan. == Overview == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] The temple was founded in 1284 (Kōan 7) by {{ill|Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja|等覚院阿闍梨日全}}, and is also referred to as Tōkaku-in.[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], pp. 522-523 It is considered the first Nichiren sect temple established in Edogawa Ward.{{efn2|The nearby {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}} was founded in 1205 (Genkyū 2) as a Shingon sect temple and converted to Nichiren Buddhism in 1288 (Shōō 1). Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|ja|日高}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐}}.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日朗}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. [[de:Yatsushiro Shrine]] [[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)-qq]] ==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)-qq]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-27]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Myokakuji Temple (Edogawa Ward)]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[ja:妙覚寺 (江戸川区)]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōkaku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan Gate.jpg | caption =
{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 6-19-10 Ichinoe, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan | latd = 35 |latm = 41 |lats = 25.6 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 52 |longs = 38.3 |longEW = E | coordinates = JP | mount = Kinshōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = 1284 (Kōan 7) | founder = Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen | alias = | cultural_properties = }} '''Myōkaku-ji''' (妙覚寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in {{ill|Ichinoe|ja|一之江}}, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kinshōzan. == Overview == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] The temple was founded in 1284 (Kōan 7) by {{ill|Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja|等覚院阿闍梨日全}}, and is also referred to as Tōkaku-in.[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], pp. 522-523 It is considered the first Nichiren sect temple established in Edogawa Ward.{{efn2|The nearby {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}} was founded in 1205 (Genkyū 2) as a Shingon sect temple and converted to Nichiren Buddhism in 1288 (Shōō 1). Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|ja|日高}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐}}.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日朗}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōkaku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan Gate.jpg | caption =
{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 6-19-10 Ichinoe, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan | latd = 35 |latm = 41 |lats = 25.6 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 52 |longs = 38.3 |longEW = E | coordinates = JP | mount = Kinshōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = 1284 (Kōan 7) | founder = Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen | alias = | cultural_properties = }} '''Myōkaku-ji''' (妙覚寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in {{ill|Ichinoe|ja|一之江}}, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kinshōzan. == Overview == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] The temple was founded in 1284 (Kōan 7) by {{ill|Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja|等覚院阿闍梨日全}}, and is also referred to as Tōkaku-in.[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], pp. 522-523 It is considered the first Nichiren sect temple established in Edogawa Ward.{{efn2|The nearby {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}} was founded in 1205 (Genkyū 2) as a Shingon sect temple and converted to Nichiren Buddhism in 1288 (Shōō 1). Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|ja|日高}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐}}.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日朗}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōkaku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan Gate.jpg | caption =
{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 6-19-10 Ichinoe, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan | latd = 35 |latm = 41 |lats = 25.6 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 52 |longs = 38.3 |longEW = E | coordinates = JP | mount = Kinshōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = 1284 (Kōan 7) | founder = Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen | alias = | cultural_properties = }} '''Myōkaku-ji''' (妙覚寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in {{ill|Ichinoe|ja|一之江}}, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kinshōzan. == Overview == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] The temple was founded in 1284 (Kōan 7) by {{ill|Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja|等覚院阿闍梨日全}}, and is also referred to as Tōkaku-in.[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], pp. 522-523 It is considered the first Nichiren sect temple established in Edogawa Ward.{{efn2|The nearby {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}} was founded in 1205 (Genkyū 2) as a Shingon sect temple and converted to Nichiren Buddhism in 1288 (Shōō 1). Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|ja|日高}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐}}.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日朗}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōkaku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan Gate.jpg | caption =

Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kinshōzan. == Overview == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] The temple was founded in 1284 (Kōan 7) by {{ill|Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja|等覚院阿闍梨日全}}, and is also referred to as Tōkaku-in.[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], pp. 522-523 It is considered the first Nichiren sect temple established in Edogawa Ward.{{efn2|The nearby {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}} was founded in 1205 (Genkyū 2) as a Shingon sect temple and converted to Nichiren Buddhism in 1288 (Shōō 1). Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|ja|日高}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐}}.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日朗}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōkaku-ji | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan Gate.jpg | caption =
{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 6-19-10 Ichinoe, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan | latd = 35 |latm = 41 |lats = 25.6 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 52 |longs = 38.3 |longEW = E | coordinates = JP | mount = Kinshōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = 1284 (Kōan 7) | founder = Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen | alias = | cultural_properties = }} '''Myōkaku-ji''' (妙覚寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|日蓮宗}} temple located in {{ill|Ichinoe|ja|一之江}}, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kinshōzan. == Overview == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] The temple was founded in 1284 (Kōan 7) by {{ill|Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja|等覚院阿闍梨日全}}, and is also referred to as Tōkaku-in.[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], pp. 522-523 It is considered the first Nichiren sect temple established in Edogawa Ward.{{efn2|The nearby {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}} was founded in 1205 (Genkyū 2) as a Shingon sect temple and converted to Nichiren Buddhism in 1288 (Shōō 1). Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|ja|日高}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐}}.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日朗}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kinshōzan. == Overview == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] The temple was founded in 1284 (Kōan 7) by {{ill|Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja|等覚院阿闍梨日全}}, and is also referred to as Tōkaku-in.[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], pp. 522-523 It is considered the first Nichiren sect temple established in Edogawa Ward.{{efn2|The nearby {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}} was founded in 1205 (Genkyū 2) as a Shingon sect temple and converted to Nichiren Buddhism in 1288 (Shōō 1). Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokkekyō-ji|ja|法華経寺}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|ja|日高}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐}}.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nikkō|ja|日朗}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken Bosatsu|ja|妙見菩薩}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#日蓮宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship.
