The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#Nichiren宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|1=長勝寺|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11651543}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|1=Q11494935|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11494935}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. ==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{translated page|ja|妙覚寺 (江戸川区)|version=97992378|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[de:Yatsushiro Shrine]] [[ja:妙覚寺 (江戸川区)]] {{Commons category|Myōkaku-ji (Ichinoe, Edogawa)}} {{wikidata link|Q54152986}} [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Temples in Edogawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[co:commons:Category:Myōkaku-ji (Ichinoe, Edogawa)]][[ja:妙覚寺 (江戸川区)]] [[co:commons:Category:Myōkaku-ji (Ichinoe, Edogawa)]][[ja:妙覚寺 (江戸川区)]] [[ja:妙覚寺 (江戸川区)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple | name = Myōkaku-ji | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Nichiren Shū|qid=Q206611|1=Nichiren-shū}} |image = File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg | caption =
{{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | location = 6-19-10 Ichinoe, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan | latd = 35 |latm = 41 |lats = 25.6 |latNS = N | longd = 139 |longm = 52 |longs = 38.3 |longEW = E | coordinates = {{Coord|35.690444444444|N|139.87730555556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | mount = Kinshōzan | sect = Nichiren | deity = | established = 1284 (Kōan 7) | founder = Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen | alias = | cultural_properties = | prefecture= {{ill|Ichinoe|qid=Q11352358|1=Ichinoe}} }} '''Myōkaku-ji''' (妙覚寺) is a {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗|1=Nichiren-shū|2=ja|3=日蓮宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiren Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q206611}} temple located in {{ill|Ichinoe|ja|一之江|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinoe|1=Ichinoe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11352358}}, Edogawa, Tokyo, Japan. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is Kinshōzan. == Overview == [[File:Myokakuji Temple,Edogawa,Tokyo,Japan.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] The temple was founded in 1284 (Kōan 7) by {{ill|Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja|等覚院阿闍梨日全|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkaku-in Ajari Nichizen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and is also referred to as Tōkaku-in.[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], pp. 522-523 It is considered the first Nichiren sect temple established in Edogawa Ward.{{efn2|The nearby {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|1=Q11494935|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11494935}} was founded in 1205 (Genkyū 2) as a Shingon sect temple and converted to Nichiren Buddhism in 1288 (Shōō 1). Another temple, {{ill|Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|妙勝寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōshō-ji (Edogawa)|1=Q11446858|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11446858}}, was initially located in Asakusa and converted in 1279 (Kōan 2).[[#江戸川区史|Edogawa Ward History (1976)]], p. 524[[#江戸川区の仏像・仏画2|Edogawa Buddhist images Vol. 2 (2013)]], pp. 19-22}} The temple's main temple affiliation is {{ill|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|en|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|qid=Q786113|lt=Hokkekyō-ji|1=Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)}} in Ichikawa, Chiba. The founder, Nichizen, was a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent military commander, and is believed to have been a disciple of either Hokkekyō-ji's second head, {{ill|Hidaka|en|Hidaka|qid=Q417203|lt=Nichikō (Hokkekyō-ji)|1=Hidaka}}, or third head, {{ill|Nichiyū|ja|日祐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiyū|1=Q11509949|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11509949}}.[[#Nichiren宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 619 The temple formerly included sub-temples such as Baibai-in, Shōunbō, and Senrinbō, and houses a wooden seated statue of Nichiren said to have been made by {{ill|Nichirō|en|Nichirō|qid=Q452631|lt=Nikkō|1=Nichirō}}, one of Nichiren's Six Senior Disciples. Two Nichiren-style paper mandalas authored by Nichiyū and Nichizen are registered as cultural assets of Edogawa Ward. Within the precincts are a bronze statue of Nichiren and a small Myōken-dō Hall dedicated to {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myōken Bosatsu|1=Myōken}}. == Founder: Nichizen == Nichizen, who founded Myōkaku-ji in May 1284, was born as a nephew of Chiba no Suke Yoritane, a prominent Kamakura-era commander. He was a disciple within the Chūzan School of Nichiren Buddhism based at Hokkekyō-ji. Nichizen is said to have studied Tendai teachings at Mount Hiei and later authored works such as "Hokke Mondō Shōgi-shō," stored at Hokkekyō-ji.[[#全国寺院名鑑|National Temple Directory (1969)]], p. Tokyo-79 == Myōken-dō == The Myōken-dō Hall venerates Myōken Bosatsu, a deity associated with the Big Dipper and believed to extend lifespans and provide protection. The hall celebrates a star festival annually on the winter solstice.{{kotobank|Myōken Bosatsu}} == Sixth Heaven Hall == Located in Ichinoe 3-chome, the Sixth Heaven Hall enshrines {{ill|Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daibutsu (Great Buddha)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and marks Nichiren's integration of Buddhist cosmology into Nichiren doctrine.[[#Nichiren宗事典|Nichiren-shū Jiten (1981)]], p. 280 == Cultural Properties == * **Paper Mandalas (by Nichiyū and Nichizen):** These historical mandalas were registered as tangible cultural assets by Edogawa Ward in 1984. * **Sixth Heaven Stone Guidepost:** This Edo-era stone marker, installed in 1826, indicates the route to the Sixth Heaven Hall. == Access == * A 10-minute walk from Ichinoe Station. == Ichinoe Nursery == Established in 1952 by Myōkaku-ji's 34th abbot, Nichishin, this nursery is a public welfare facility under the management of Myōkaku-ji.{{Cite web|url=https://ichinoe-hoikuen.jp/|title=Ichinoe Nursery Official Website|work=Ichinoe Nursery|publisher=Myōkaku-ji|accessdate=2018-04-15}} == Related Nichiren Temples == * {{ill|Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|長勝寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōshō-ji (Edogawa)|1=長勝寺|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11651543}}, a former sub-temple of Myōkaku-ji. * {{ill|Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|ja|感応寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannō-ji (Edogawa)|1=Q11494935|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11494935}}, which transitioned to Nichiren Buddhism under Nichizen's mentorship. ==References== {{reflist}} {{Myōken Faith}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{translated page|ja|妙覚寺 (江戸川区)|version=97992378|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[de:Yatsushiro Shrine]] [[ja:妙覚寺 (江戸川区)]] {{Commons category|Myōkaku-ji (Ichinoe, Edogawa)}} {{wikidata link|Q54152986}} [[Category:Nichiren-shū temples]] [[Category:Temples in Edogawa, Tokyo]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
